1.synthesis: Psalm 104 is the great creation hymn of the Psalter, tracing the structure of Genesis 1 while transforming the cosmic imagery of the ancient Near East into doxology. YHWH is clothed with light as a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent (v. 2); he rides on the cherub-chariot of clouds and makes winds his messengers and flames his ministers (vv. 3–4; cf. Ps 18:10). The sea is rebuked to its boundaries (vv. 7–9) and from there waters go to nourish land and creature. The moon marks the mo’adim (appointed festivals, v. 19). Most striking is the Leviathan—elsewhere a symbol of chaos—recast as a creature YHWH “formed to play in” the sea (v. 26), frolicsome rather than threatening. The creation cycle reaches its theological summit at verse 30: “When you send forth your Spirit (ruach), they are created, and you renew the face of the ground,” echoing the Spirit hovering over the waters in Genesis 1:2.
1.historical_context: Psalm 104 shares remarkable verbal parallels with the Egyptian Great Hymn to the Aten (fourteenth century BC), particularly in its celebration of the sun providing light and warmth for all creatures. The parallels illuminate the comparative context but also the radical differences: where the Aten hymn ascribes divinity to the solar disk, Psalm 104 makes the sun a mere timekeeper YHWH created (v. 19). The demythologization is total: Leviathan, the chaos monster of Ugaritic myth, is a creature YHWH made to frolic in the sea. The Sinai theophanic tradition also appears: the mountains tremble, smoke rises when YHWH touches them (v. 32), evoking Exodus 19:18.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 104 follows a rough Day 1–6 structure mirroring Genesis 1. Days 1–2 (light, sky, waters): vv. 1–9. Days 3–4 (land, plants, sun and moon): vv. 10–23. Days 5–6 (sea creatures, land animals): vv. 24–30. Each section shows not merely that YHWH created these things but that he actively sustains them: the young lions roar for their prey and seek their food from God (v. 21). The creation is not a static artifact but an ongoing act of divine provision. The hiddenness of YHWH’s face (v. 29) means death; the sending of his Spirit (v. 30) means life and renewal.
1.theological_application: Psalm 104 teaches that creation is not a stage on which the human drama plays out but a sustained act of YHWH’s providential care for every creature. The lions and the stork and Leviathan are all included in the psalm’s vision of a creation YHWH attends to. The Spirit’s role in renewal (v. 30) grounds the theology of new creation: the same Spirit who hovered over the void at the beginning (Gen 1:2) continuously renews what age and decay diminish. Communities shaped by this psalm will have a theology of creation care rooted not in ecological anxiety but in wonder at YHWH’s intimate provision for all that he has made.
1.christology: The New Testament applies the creation-sustaining work of Psalm 104 to Christ. Colossians 1:17 declares that “in him all things hold together”—the active sustenance of Psalm 104 (YHWH’s hands opening to give food in due season, v. 28; his Spirit renewing the face of the earth, v. 30) is the work of the one through whom and for whom all things were created (Col 1:16). The “new creation” of 2 Corinthians 5:17 is the eschatological renewal that Psalm 104:30 prefigures: the Spirit who renews the face of the ground ultimately renews all things in Christ, bringing the entire creation to its destined glory (Rom 8:21).
